name: David Martínez-Rivera
institution: University of Michigan, Autonomous Systems Lab
backstory: David is in his third year and feeling the pressure. He passed his qualifying exam, has solid coursework, and co-authored two workshop papers with his labmates — but he still hasn't found *his* research problem. His advisor keeps saying 'you need to own a problem,' but David finds everything either already solved or impossibly hard. He's read hundreds of papers across manipulation, planning, and human-robot interaction, but each time he gets excited about a direction, he finds a 2023 paper that already did it. He's started to wonder if all the 'good problems' are taken. His funding runs out in two years, and he watches first-year students (like Maya) arrive with clear, passionate research visions while he still feels directionless. David is technically strong — he can implement any paper he reads and has become the lab's go-to person for debugging ROS and simulation issues. But he privately worries he's becoming a 'research engineer' rather than a 'researcher.' He comes from a family of engineers in Guadalajara and is the first in his family to pursue a PhD. The pressure to justify years of foregone industry salary weighs on him.

anxiety: His advisor asking 'So what's your thesis about?'
Seeing peers submit papers while he's still searching for a topic
Reading yet another paper that 'scoops' an idea he was considering
Comparing his progress to students who started at the same time
motivation: Finding a genuine gap in the literature that no one has addressed
Having a conversation where a half-formed idea clicks into focus
Realizing his broad reading gives him a unique cross-cutting perspective
Building something that actually works on a real robot
His advisor saying 'that's interesting, tell me more'
frustration: Vague advice like 'just find what excites you'
Papers that overclaim novelty for incremental work
Feeling like breadth of knowledge is undervalued compared to depth
The sense that he's 'wasting time' by exploring rather than committing

existingStrengths: Exceptional breadth of knowledge — can discuss manipulation, planning, HRI, and perception with depth
Strong implementation skills — has reproduced 10+ papers and built custom simulation environments
Excellent at identifying flaws in existing work during paper reviews
Natural systems thinker — sees connections between subfields others miss
Reliable collaborator — has helped 4 labmates debug their projects substantially
Good writer when he has clarity on what to say
weaknesses: Paralyzed by the paradox of choice — too many interesting directions, can't commit
Confuses 'someone worked on this topic' with 'this problem is solved'
Reads papers to find existing solutions rather than reading for gaps and limitations
Undervalues his cross-cutting perspective as a research strength
Avoids writing until he feels he has a 'complete' idea (perfectionism disguised as preparation)
Doesn't keep structured notes — his insights are scattered across bookmarks and half-finished documents

successLooksLike.shortTerm: David shifts from 'searching for a problem' to 'evaluating candidate problems.' He generates 3-5 concrete research questions, each with a clear gap, a feasible approach, and a connection to his strengths. He writes a one-page pitch for each.
successLooksLike.mediumTerm: David commits to one research direction and writes a 5-page research proposal his advisor approves. He starts preliminary experiments and submits an abstract to a workshop. He stops seeing exploration as wasted time and starts seeing it as his competitive advantage.
successLooksLike.longTerm: David's thesis combines insights from multiple subfields — the kind of work that only someone with his breadth could produce. He publishes at ICRA or RSS, and reviewers note the 'novel cross-disciplinary framing.' He helps other students find their problems by asking the questions his advisor couldn't.

interactionPreferences.tone: Direct, intellectually engaging, peer-level. Don't sugarcoat — he respects honesty. But distinguish between 'honest' and 'dismissive.'
interactionPreferences.pacing: Can move fast on concepts. Slow down on emotional/motivational aspects — he deflects vulnerability with technical talk.
interactionPreferences.feedback: Frame his breadth as an asset, not a symptom. Challenge him to read papers for gaps instead of solutions. Push him to write early and ugly rather than waiting for perfection.
avoidPhrases: It doesn't matter what you choose
You should have figured this out by now
